channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Rubio’s Absence From Iran Talks Highlights Stay-at-Home Role
item.description: President Trump has outsourced much of his diplomacy to others, while Mr. Rubio focuses on his second job as national security adviser.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: As President Trump prepares to send a delegation to the latest round of U.S.-Iran talks in Pakistan, his secretary of state, Marco Rubio, will remain where he often does: at home.

item.title: How Jet Fuel Shortages Could Affect Summer Travel to Europe and Beyond
item.description: Facing sky-high fuel costs linked to the war in Iran, airlines are cutting routes and raising prices. European vacations are looking a lot less affordable.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kirill Kudryavtsev/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The German airline Lufthansa cut nearly 20,000 flights over the next six months to conserve jet fuel, which could begin to run low in Europe as soon as next month.

item.title: The ‘Lasting Damage’ of Pirro’s Investigation of the Federal Reserve and Powell
item.description: The Trump administration’s attacks on the Federal Reserve have rattled confidence in the central bank’s ability to operate independently before a leadership transition.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump visited the Federal Reserve’s Washington headquarters in July during renovations.

item.title: Justice Department Drops Criminal Investigation of Fed Chair Jerome Powell
item.description: The decision to end the inquiry into Jerome H. Powell’s handling of the Federal Reserve’s renovation could allow Kevin M. Warsh, the president’s pick for Fed chair, to be confirmed.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith, Tony Romm and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell, the Federal Reserve chair.

item.title: Trump Says He Dislikes Prediction Markets. His Family Invests in Them.
item.description: The White House has warned staff not to wager on government decisions, but his family’s involvement with these firms undermines the president’s message.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s eldest son, Donald Trump Jr., has ties to two of the industry’s top prediction market firms, including Polymarket, a platform that prosecutors say a U.S. soldier used to place bets using classified information.

item.title: Gambling Companies Celebrate Indictments as Pressure Grows
item.description: Gambling companies have cited recent federal charges as proof that illegal activity is being rooted out. The companies’ critics say the cases do not address widespread illicit activity.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich, Santul Nerkar, Meredith Honig and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times

item.title: What Are Prediction Markets, and Why Are They Causing Controversy?
item.description: The indictment of a soldier who bet on the U.S. operation to capture President Nicolás Maduro of Venezuela put renewed focus on a new way to gamble, and a new way to cheat.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:credit: Jenny Kane/Associated Press
item.media:description: Prediction markets like Kalshi and Polymarket are a growing phenomenon.

item.title: U.S. Says Venezuelan Government Can Pay for Nicolás Maduro’s Defense
item.description: The issue had been hanging over the former Venezuelan leader’s federal criminal case for weeks. Last month, a judge indicated that he was skeptical of the U.S. government’s rationale for blocking the funds.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Nicolás Maduro being escorted from a helicopter to be taken to the federal courthouse in Manhattan in January.

item.title: Inside Trump and David Ellison’s Private Party in Washington
item.description: The gathering included executives and leading journalists from CBS News, which Paramount owns, and the head of the Justice Department, which is reviewing the acquisition.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon/Reuters

item.title: Appeals Court Says Trump’s Ban on Asylum Claims at Border Is Illegal
item.description: The ruling could require the Trump administration to begin processing new applications from asylum seekers at the southern border.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Alejandro Cegarra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Migrants approved for asylum appointments with Customs and Border Protection last year in Tapachula, Mexico.

item.title: Trump Administration Approves Firing Squad Executions for Death Penalty
item.description: The Justice Department also reauthorized the use of a death penalty drug, and will seek to shorten the length of some legal appeals.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett and Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department has reauthorized the use of pentobarbital in executions of federal inmates and will also allow the use of firing squads and “additional methods of execution.”

item.title: It Was Just a Podcast. Now, It’s Kelce Land.
item.description: When Amazon gutted its podcast company, it built a new department that made creators kings, starting with the football stars Jason and Travis Kelce.
item.dc:creator: Jessica Testa
item.media:credit: Balazs Gardi for The New York Times

item.title: The 85-Year-Old Widow Snagged by Trump’s Immigration Crackdown
item.description: In her first interview since being deported, Marie-Thérèse Ross-Mahé, the French widow of a former G.I., recounted her experience in ICE detention.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times

item.title: Camp Mystic May Not Receive License to Reopen After Fatal Flood in Texas
item.description: A review by state officials in Texas said the camp, hit by catastrophic flooding last year, was not in compliance with safety requirements.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman and Ruth Graham
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: Some of Camp Mystic’s emergency plans were judged incomplete, insufficient or missing by the Texas Department of State Health Services.

item.title: Deb Haaland’s Historic Run for New Mexico Governor Hits a Primary Snag
item.description: In New Mexico, the former interior secretary seemed to have an easy path to becoming the first Native American woman elected governor — until Sam Bregman challenged her.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times
item.media:description: Deb Haaland, former Interior secretary and congresswoman, seemed like a shoo-in for New Mexico governor, but she finds herself in a real Democratic primary race.

item.title: Republicans’ Likely Strategy as Midterms Near: Negative Campaigning
item.description: Republicans are likely to fall back on a tried-and-tested strategy for the midterms: Going negative.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times

item.title: A New Idea to Save the AMOC? Dam the Bering Strait.
item.description: Blocking the narrow waterway between Russia and Alaska could help stabilize a vulnerable system of ocean currents, scientists found in a study.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:credit: NASA
item.media:description: A satellite view of the Bering Strait, which separates northeastern Russia and Alaska, in summer.

item.title: How Dermatologists Are Helping People Who’ve Been Sex Trafficked
item.description: People who are sex trafficked are often branded with names and symbols. Free removal programs are helping them move on.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: For 17 years, Kathy Givens carried her trafficker’s name on her back — a lingering symbol of trauma and ownership. Watching it fade, she said, felt like getting pieces of her dignity back.

item.title: SiriusXM Said to Be in Early Talks to Acquire iHeartMedia
item.description: A deal, which was described by two people familiar with the discussions, would unite two major providers of traditional and satellite radio.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch and Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Amy Lombard for The New York Times
item.media:description: The hosts of “SmartLess,” from left, Jason Bateman, Will Arnett and Sean Hayes, on a sofa after a taping in 2024. SiriusXM’s chief executive, Jennifer C. Witz, and chief content officer, Scott Greenstein, stood behind them.

item.title: Were Neanderthals Able to Hunt Elephants? The Proof Is in an Ancient Bone
item.description: A new study found that a pachyderm skeleton, dismissed for decades as unimportant, offers evidence of careful planning, teamwork and a calculated kill.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz

item.title: The Conspiracy Theory Behind Tucker Carlson’s Apology
item.description: Rather than honestly reckoning with their role in America’s derangement, MAGA apostates are creating a scapegoat to explain it away.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg

item.title: Stewart Brand on the Ideals the Tech Industry Forgot
item.description: The counterculture icon Stewart Brand is advocating values that are more countercultural than ever.
item.dc:creator: Ezra Klein and Annie Galvin
item.media:credit: John Anderson/Getty Images

item.title: Kenyan Court Strikes Down Ruling Protecting Right to Abortion
item.description: The decision, in a country where thousands of women die yearly from unsafe abortions, held that abortions deprive unborn children of the “right to life.”
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.media:credit: Simon Maina/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Wayne Moss, Guitarist Who Helped Broaden Country Sound, Dies at 88
item.description: A top Nashville musician, he played on Bob Dylan’s “I Want You,” Roy Orbison’s “Oh, Pretty Woman” and Dolly Parton’s “Jolene.”
item.dc:creator: Bill Friskics-Warren

item.title: One of Two Missing USF Doctoral Students Is Found Dead, Officials Say
item.description: The University of South Florida students were reported missing last week. A roommate of the student whose body was found was charged in connection with his disappearance.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:credit: Hillsborough County Sheriff's Office, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A search continued on Friday for a missing University of South Florida doctoral students after the body of another student who had been reported missing was found on a bridge over Old Tampa Bay, officials said.

item.title: 31 Sloths Acquired by an Orlando Animal Attraction Died, Officials Say
item.description: Many of the deaths were attributed to a lack of heat at an unauthorized warehouse that Sloth World was using, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Fox 35 Orlando
item.media:description: The sloths were going to be the latest attraction at Sloth World in Orlando, Fla.
